name: OC Purple Accountability
tagline: Every vote. Every meeting. Every margin matters.
mission:
Orange County is one of America's few genuinely purple counties. Voter registration: 36.4% D, 33.9% R, 23.3% NPP. The City of Orange is split 36.6% D to 34.8% R. The 2024 CD-45 race was decided by 581 votes. In this environment, information is power.

OC Purple Accountability tracks voting records and policy positions across Orange County's key governing bodies — the Board of Supervisors, City of Orange council, City of Irvine council, and state legislative delegation. We publish scorecards. We distribute candidate questionnaires. We attend meetings and track who votes how.

We exist because most OC residents have no idea what their elected officials actually do. They might know who they voted for, but they don't know how that person voted on the housing element, the county budget, or the homelessness plan. This information asymmetry benefits organized special interests and hurts everyone else.

The 2026 elections are unusually high-stakes: the Board of Supervisors majority (currently 3-2 D) hangs on Districts 4 and 5. Multiple open state legislative seats (SD-34, AD-68) are in play. City councils in both Orange and Irvine have consequential races. New congressional maps from Prop 50 reshape federal representation. And the Governor's race (Newsom termed out) determines who enforces California's housing mandates.

In close elections, informed voters are the most powerful political force. We make voters informed.
theory_of_change: In close elections, information asymmetry benefits organized special interests. Scorecards and questionnaires level the playing field by making voting records and policy positions accessible to every voter, not just insiders. When voters know what their representatives actually do — not just what they say — accountability follows. In a county where margins are razor-thin, even small improvements in voter information shift outcomes. A candidate who knows their votes are being tracked and published votes differently. A voter who sees a scorecard votes more deliberately. We create the feedback loop between government action and public knowledge that democracy requires but rarely has.

political_positioning.description: Centered and deliberately nonpartisan in framing. The accountability mission appeals across the political spectrum — conservatives want government transparency, progressives want officials held to their promises, and moderates want data-driven evaluation. Our scorecards measure actions against stated positions, not against an ideological standard. A Republican who votes consistently with their platform gets a high consistency score, same as a Democrat who does. The compass cloud is intentionally wide because our coalition spans the spectrum. We are not a partisan organization — we are a transparency organization that operates in a purple county.

key_policy_positions: Publish regular scorecards for all OC Board of Supervisors members tracking votes on key issues
Develop and distribute candidate questionnaires for all 2026 races — county, city, state, and federal
Track meeting attendance and voting records for City of Orange and City of Irvine councils
Make all scorecards and tracking data publicly available in accessible, shareable formats
Maintain nonpartisan framing — evaluate officials against their own stated positions and constituent interests
Use public records requests to obtain voting records, campaign finance data, and policy positions
Partner with Voice of OC and other media to amplify accountability findings
Host candidate forums in partnership with League of Women Voters Orange Coast
Track campaign finance and major donor influence across OC races
Publish election guides summarizing candidate positions for every race on OC ballots

active_campaigns.title: Board of Supervisors Majority Defense
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (April 16, 2026): Both races have known candidate fields. ⚡ CRITICAL THIS WEEK: April 23 finance filing deadline — new fundraising data will reveal spending trajectories heading into the final 6 weeks before the June 2 primary. 47 DAYS until primary.

District 4 (open seat — Chaffee termed out, 341,494 voters: 40.4% D, 30.5% R, 23.1% NPP): Fred Jung (Fullerton Mayor, $354K cash on hand, endorsed by Fullerton firefighters & police unions — re-registered as No Party Preference after leaving Dem Party), Connor Traut (Buena Park Vice Mayor, $225K, endorsed by OC Democratic Party, Chaffee, OC Professional Firefighters, carpenters/electricians unions), Rose Espinoza ($164K raised, $150K is self-loan, La Habra council, endorsed by LA County Fire Fighters Local 1014), Tim Shaw ($82K raised including $5K self-loan, endorsed by OC Republican Party). Top-two from June 2 primary advance to November.

District 5 (436,228 voters: 38.1% R, 32.8% D, 22.1% NPP): Incumbent Supervisor Katrina Foley (D, $285K cash on hand) vs. Assemblywoman Diane Dixon (R, $135K cash, outraised Foley by $50K in H2 2025, endorsed by Senators Strickland and Choi). Foley won 51-49 in 2022. Kamala Harris won D5 by just 318 votes in 2024. NOTE: If either candidate wins over 50% in the primary, they avoid a November runoff.

The board currently has a 3-2 Democratic majority. If Democrats lose EITHER seat, the board flips — affecting the $10.8B annual budget, homelessness policy (including the new anti-camping ordinance), transit funding, and housing policy countywide.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Board of Supervisors races are low-information, low-turnout elections where organized advocacy has outsized impact. Most voters can't name their supervisor. A clear, nonpartisan voter guide that evaluates candidates on specific policy positions — housing, budget, homelessness, transit — helps voters make informed choices in a race they'd otherwise skip or vote randomly. When the margin of victory is measured in thousands of votes (or less), even modest improvements in informed voting shift outcomes.
next_actions: Profile all four District 4 candidates (Jung, Traut, Espinoza, Shaw) — backgrounds, positions, endorsements, donors
Deep-dive District 5: compare Foley's record as supervisor vs Dixon's Assembly voting record on housing, transit, budget
Develop and distribute candidate questionnaire to all six candidates across both districts
Create voter guide comparing candidates on housing, budget, homelessness, transit, and transparency — publish by May 1
Track campaign finance filings — Jung's $197K war chest vs others creates an early narrative worth publicizing
Coordinate with Voice of OC to amplify candidate evaluation and campaign finance coverage
Organize candidate forum in partnership with League of Women Voters Orange Coast — target late April/early May
Publish final voter guide at least 4 weeks before June 2 primary

active_campaigns.title: City of Orange Council Scorecard
active_campaigns.summary: Track and publish the voting records of all City of Orange council members on key issues — housing (especially Housing Element compliance and Builder's Remedy projects), budget, development, and public safety. The scorecard will be published before the November 2026 elections when 3 council seats and the mayor are on the ballot. Orange's nonpartisan elections make information even more critical — without party labels, voters rely on name recognition or random choice. A scorecard breaks through that.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: In nonpartisan city elections, voters have even less information than in partisan races. A well-researched scorecard that tracks actual votes (not campaign promises) gives voters concrete data to evaluate incumbents and compare them to challengers. Council members who know their votes are being tracked and published have an incentive to take positions publicly rather than hiding behind procedural votes.
next_actions: Obtain City of Orange council meeting minutes for the past 12 months
Identify key votes on housing, budget, and development issues
Design scorecard methodology — what votes to include, how to weight them
Research November 2026 council candidate field (3 seats + mayor)
Draft scorecard format for public distribution
Partner with Voice of OC for coverage of scorecard findings

active_campaigns.title: Irvine Council Scorecard
active_campaigns.summary: Track and publish voting records for Irvine City Council, especially on the General Plan update (57,656 units proposed), Irvine Company-related decisions, and Great Park development. The 5-2 Democratic majority is at risk in November 2026 (3 seats + mayor on ballot). Irvine's decisions on housing density affect the entire OC housing market. The Irvine Company's monopoly power makes transparency especially important — track council votes on matters where the Irvine Company has a financial interest.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: The Irvine Company's near-monopoly over Irvine's apartment market creates unique transparency needs. When one entity controls ~75% of a city's apartment units, every council vote on development policy is a potential conflict of interest. A public scorecard tracking how council members vote on Irvine Company-related items exposes these dynamics. Voters can then evaluate whether their representatives serve residents or developers.
next_actions: Research Irvine council voting patterns on General Plan and Irvine Company matters
Identify Irvine Company-related agenda items over the past 12 months
Design scorecard methodology tracking housing, Irvine Company, and budget votes
Track November 2026 Irvine council candidates' positions and funding sources
Coordinate with People for Housing OC on data sharing

active_campaigns.title: State Legislative Open-Seat Tracking
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (April 16, 2026): Candidate fields are set for both legislative seats, and the Governor's race continues its post-Swalwell upheaval.

SD-34 (Umberg termed out): Straight D-vs-R contest: Avelino Valencia (D, current AD-68 Assemblymember) vs. Rhonda Shader (R, former Placentia mayor, 2022 runner-up). Both will advance from the June 2 top-two primary to the November general.

AD-68 (Valencia running for Senate): Jessie Lopez (D, Santa Ana councilor, endorsed by CA Legislative Women's Caucus, raised $73,737) vs. David Peñaloza (D, Santa Ana councilor, endorsed by Valencia, raised $142,897). All-Democratic field means the primary decides the seat.

GOVERNOR'S RACE — RAPIDLY EVOLVING:
• Swalwell dropped out April 12 (sexual misconduct), resigned from Congress April 13-14. Name stays on June 2 ballot.
• NEW (April 15): Gov. Newsom facing intense pressure to intervene — has spoken to Pelosi, Schiff and Padilla advisers in discussions. But Newsom has decided to let the race play out, not endorsing. Democrats who endorsed Swalwell feel 'embarrassed' and are cautious.
• NEW: Nexstar/KTLA5 commissioned a new Emerson College poll (in field this week) to reset the April 22 debate lineup. 5% threshold for qualification. Confirmed qualifiers: Hilton (R), Bianco (R), Porter (D), Steyer (D). Results and final lineup expected by end of this week.
• Post-Swalwell polls: ABC 10News shows Steyer leading; Porter and Steyer biggest beneficiaries of Swalwell exit. But Democratic fragmentation persists — early ballots go out in 3 weeks.
• The R-vs-R November scenario is now the most realistic path without a consolidation endorsement.

April 23 campaign finance filing deadline applies to all state races — new data expected this week.

State legislators shape California's housing mandate framework (RHNA, Builder's Remedy, SB 9/10 enforcement) that drives the core YIMBY fight in OC.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: State legislative races, especially primaries, are low-information elections where candidate questionnaires and voter guides have disproportionate influence. Most voters know nothing about state assembly candidates. A clear comparison of positions on housing, transit, and local government policy helps voters choose representatives who will strengthen (or weaken) the state mandate framework that OC housing advocacy depends on.
next_actions: Profile SD-34 candidates: Valencia (D) vs Shader (R) — compare housing mandate positions, Assembly voting records, Shader's 2022 run
Profile AD-68 candidates: Lopez (D) and Peñaloza (D) — compare Santa Ana council voting records on housing and development
Add Governor's race to tracking — evaluate candidates on housing enforcement posture (RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals)
Develop questionnaire focused on housing mandates, RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy, transit, and local control
Distribute questionnaires to SD-34, AD-68, and Governor candidates and publish all responses
Create comparison guides for all three races — SD-34, AD-68, and Governor
Track campaign finance filings — next filing April 23
Publish voter guides at least 4 weeks before June 2 primary

aligned_organizations.name: League of Women Voters Orange Coast
aligned_organizations.relationship: Primary partner for candidate forums and voter education. Nonpartisan credibility strengthens our accountability framing.

aligned_organizations.name: Voice of OC
aligned_organizations.relationship: Essential media partner. Their investigative journalism provides source material for scorecards, and our tracking data feeds their coverage.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Housing Now
aligned_organizations.relationship: Housing-focused ally. Our scorecards track housing votes; their advocacy uses the data to pressure non-compliant cities.

aligned_organizations.name: People for Housing OC
aligned_organizations.relationship: YIMBY ally — our housing vote tracking supports their advocacy campaigns.

aligned_organizations.name: Indivisible OC
aligned_organizations.relationship: Progressive grassroots ally. Their voter mobilization capacity amplifies our voter guide distribution.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Family formation policy depends on elected officials who support housing and childcare infrastructure. Our scorecards help identify allies.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Scorecards shared on social media, Voice of OC coverage, election guide distribution through partner organizations. OC's razor-thin election margins make our data newsworthy.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Read a scorecard, attend a candidate forum, check your representatives' voting records. Understand how your city compares on housing and budget transparency.
engagement_pipeline.action: Vote informed using our guides. Share scorecards with neighbors. Attend a board or council meeting to observe your representatives in action. Submit public records requests. Contact your representatives about specific votes.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the research team tracking votes and building scorecards. Lead tracking for a specific jurisdiction (City of Orange, Irvine, or Board of Supervisors). Coordinate candidate forums. Manage campaign finance monitoring.

immediate.action: Research all declared candidates for Board of Supervisors Districts 4 and 5
Design candidate questionnaire for all 2026 races
Contact League of Women Voters Orange Coast for forum co-sponsorship
Begin collecting City of Orange council meeting minutes for scorecard

medium_term.action: Distribute questionnaires to all BoS D4/D5 and state legislative candidates
Publish Board of Supervisors voter guide for June 2 primary
Publish City of Orange council scorecard v1
Host candidate forum for BoS District 4 candidates

strategic.action: Publish comprehensive November 2026 election guide covering City of Orange, City of Irvine, and all OC races
strategic.notes: This is the capstone product — a complete voter guide for every OC race that matters. Coordinate with all partner organizations for maximum distribution.

strategic.action: Establish ongoing council vote tracking infrastructure for permanent accountability
strategic.notes: Elections are episodic but accountability should be permanent. Build systems (volunteer rotation, data pipeline, publication schedule) that outlast individual election cycles.

strategic.action: Expand coverage to OCTA, school boards, and water districts in 2027
strategic.notes: Start with the highest-stakes bodies (BoS, city councils, state legislature) and expand as capacity allows. Special districts fly under the radar but make consequential decisions.
